chapters.title: Deep work is becoming rarer by the day
chapters.text:
Deep work is the ability to focus without distraction on cognitively demanding tasks. The deep work hypothesis states that this skill is becoming rare but increasingly valuable in our economy. Therefore, those who cultivate this skill will become irreplaceable.

So, we know deep work is scarce, but why?

Well, shallow work is much easier, and even large businesses encourage it. Facebook, for instance, wants to launch the most extensive open floor plan in the world. More than three thousand employees will work on movable furniture spread over a ten-acre office space.

This design stimulates constant connectivity and fast response times, encouraging employees to choose the path of least resistance. It is a shallow work-induced environment where the employees work superficially.

Most workers engage in visible "busyness" or "busyness as a proxy for productivity." Many do not know what it means and how to become valuable at work, so they indulge in several industrial productivity indicators — visibly doing things.

Deep work is valuable because it maximizes the amount of productivity you can obtain from a specific period.

Deep work, on the other hand, entails single-tasking with no distractions during intense focus. As a result, you boost your performance and productivity.

Studies have found that multitasking makes people less productive and that focusing on a single task maximizes effectual output. Although a lack of deep work allows the company to uncover significant potential advantages, it can also cause the company to miss a concrete way to optimize its production.

If this trend continues, deep work will keep increasing in value; therefore, you can use the following advice to develop your ability to perform deep work and reap bountiful rewards.

chapters.title: Deep work‘s value is in its rarity
chapters.text:
Deep work is any professional activity carried out in a distraction-free state, allowing individuals to use their cognitive capabilities to the maximum. On the other hand, work can be shallow. It comes as non-cognitive demanding tasks, often carried out while distracted. Shallow work is rarely valuable, and those who do this kind of work are often replaceable.

Deep work requires an environment free of distractions to maximize your concentration. Shallow work is best for low cognitive thinking tasks, such as checking your email or responding to notifications. It is an almost automatic, replicable process that doesn't require focus and provides satisfaction from completing simple tasks.

If you don't produce, you won't thrive — no matter how skilled or talented you are. ~ Cal Newport

Deep work is increasingly rare, leading to a rise in its value. But if you can cultivate this skill, you will thrive.

As skills and jobs become obsolete due to automation, many people will lose out and won’t survive. In the same vein, the groups below will thrive and excel:

• The highly skilled workers. Those adept at communicating and interacting with complex machines take away many low-skilled positions.
• The superstars know that being the best in a market is not enough. Yes, there is a premium for being the best, but when the market is flooded with performers, customers will only go for the best of the best, no matter how small their competitive advantage over others.
• The owners have colossal capital and invest it in emerging technologies that threaten to disrupt the way we live.

Technology is ever-changing, and you must quickly learn and relearn a never-ending language.

To become a leader in this new economy dominated by machines and automation, you need to refine these two core abilities:

• The ability to quickly learn hard things: Technology is changing so rapidly that it is increasingly more challenging to grasp which innovation is at the forefront. The competition uses complicated command lines, such as SQL. It is imperative to stay up-to-date. Try this practical step: Pick one new technical skill relevant to your field and commit to 90 minutes of focused learning daily for the next month.
• The ability to quickly and consistently produce high-quality work. Remaining flexible to change, no matter how drastic, and maintaining an open mind will take you to the top. An outdated viewpoint will hinder you.

chapters.title: Avoid work in a semi-distracted state
chapters.text:
When you switch from task A to task B, 100% of your attention doesn’t automatically go to the new task; you might remain stuck on the previous assignment. This phenomenon is called attention residue, which is strongest if task A was unbounded or low-intensity in nature. Even if you complete task A before switching, part of your attention will temporarily remain on it.

For instance, what's wrong with occasionally checking your email? Some say it’s better than always leaving it open.

However, it’s hardly an improvement: as you switch to your inbox, you create a new target for your attention. If you cannot accomplish task B, you will switch back to task A, with task B left unfinished.

Attention residue hinders productivity when you work deep for an extended time.

The principle of attention residue works like this: Every time you switch from task A to B, some of your attention remains in task A. Concentrating on the new task is more challenging, so you lose some productivity when switching jobs.

During an experiment, two groups of participants worked on a set of puzzles. The first group was interrupted and distracted from the task with an engaging activity — assessing CVs and picking a potential candidate. Participants of the second group were allowed to start and finish the puzzle without distractions. Researchers tested the two groups to determine if attention residue was present, and the findings confirmed that attention residue hinders performance on the following task.

The quality of work depends on how long you work and on your focus. Regardless of your talent, those who learn deep work will outperform you. However, some roles don’t benefit from this type of work.

For example, an executive is better off delegating a task to three employees who will contemplate it deeply while handling more essential parts of the business. Some salesmen have to remain connected with people to seize opportunities and respond to clients. But don’t be quick to disregard deep focus from your workflow.

Experiment and see if connectivity is as essential in your job as you believe. You might find it’s not worth dispersing your attention; the emails can wait.

If you want to perform at an optimum productivity level, work for long periods on a single task with full concentration and zero distraction — work deeply!

chapters.title: The rules of deep work
chapters.text:
Now that you know how valuable deep work is for productivity, you will realize that knowledge is quite easy. Doing is the hard part. Humans are terrible at multitasking because we are easily distracted. The following rules will teach you how to incorporate deep work into your life:

• Work deeply
• Embrace boredom
• Quit social media
• Drain the shallows

Rule 1

Work deeply. Due to the amount of effort working deep requires, many avoid it. Considering our environment's culture, it becomes even more challenging.

We have finite willpower. As it is depleted, we tend to favor shallow work.

Rule 2

Embrace boredom. Acute concentration is a skill you should cultivate. Just as athletes take optimal care of their bodies outside training sessions, you need to hone your concentration skills outside deep work sessions.

You probably get repeatedly stuck in a vicious cycle of distractions, even when you want to work deeply. To prevent this issue, embrace boredom! Stop yourself from checking your phone notifications every time there is a beep. Train your ability to resist distractions. Put your phone in another room during your next work session. Notice when you instinctively reach for it — that's your distraction addiction talking.

Rule 3

Quit social media. Social media platforms are prime examples of shallow living. They offer nothing beneficial in return for all the time we give them. It will be a barrier to working deep if you feel the urge to check your social media account every few minutes. Due to its addictive tendencies, deep work and social media aren’t compatible. Try this: Take a 30-day social media break. Delete apps from your phone and see how it makes you feel.

Rule 4

Drain the shallows. The Shallows, a book by Nicholas Carr, depicts the adverse effects of the Internet on our mental life. Examples of shallow work include: attending meetings, making phone calls, answering emails, and many other low-value activities.

If you want to work deep, you need to drain the shallows. Create time for deep work, and spend little time doing shallow work.

chapters.title: Stick to routines to ritualize deep work
chapters.text:
Engaging in deep work in a world that encourages shallow work is challenging. We can benefit from establishing rituals and routines to reduce the willpower needed to transition into a session of unbroken concentration.

Four broad approaches can help you carve time for deep work. Do not rely on shortcuts. They are less effective because they demand high willpower and are not sustainable.

Choose any of the following approaches, or mix two or more to fit your lifestyle:

• Monastic: Cutting yourself off from distractions completely is similar to monks in a monastery. For example, book a library study room for three days, turn off your phone completely, bring only water and healthy snacks, and work 8-hour focused sessions with 30-minute breaks.
• Bimodal: Alternates between living an everyday engaged life and a monastic one. To do this, spend weekdays handling meetings and emails, then dedicate entire weekends to deep work with zero interruptions.
• Rhythmic: Involves setting a fixed time for deep work every day. For instance, wake at 5.30 am daily, have coffee by 6.00 am, and conduct deep work from 6.30 – 8.30 am before emails touch your consciousness.
• Journalistic. Fitting deep work into your schedule anytime you can, similar to a journalist ready to write a piece on a deadline when needed. Create a "deep work kit," including noise-canceling headphones, project files, and a 15-minute transition ritual you can deploy whenever you have an unexpected hour free.

A monastic philosophy gives you long, uninterrupted periods to work. You can free yourself from minor concerns and postpone them until you finish a task. This radical approach might not work for those who must complete daily or weekly chores. However, this philosophy may lead you to a great career if deadlines do not limit you.

According to the bimodal approach, you can focus on deep work once you’ve had your time of distractions. You divide your time from a scale of a week to a whole season into deep work and free time.

Adopting the rhythmic method means making deep work a habit. Start at the same time every day. Keeping a visual aid, like crossing a day in the calendar, can ease the process.

The journalistic approach works once you gain experience with deep work, since you need to switch to deep work immediately. Your "deep work kit" becomes essential — having everything ready means no wasted time gathering materials or getting into the right mindset. But when you get used to concentrating, you can work whenever you have the time.

Being strict with your schedule is critical for success.

Many successful people say that discipline helps them focus on their work. While there’s no one correct way to work, these approaches can guide you to bring deep work into your life.

Did you know? A famous cyberpunk author, Neal Stephenson, is impossible to reach. He doesn’t list an email address anywhere because of his monastic approach to writing.

chapters.title: The value of systematic idleness
chapters.text:
You should have regular and substantial leisure time when you are free from work. This organized idleness will help you get deep work done. After all, no one can always focus on work, so you need to get your tasks out of your head regularly. Here’s why:

• Downtime aids insights: Your unconscious will take care of any lingering issues you have. Therefore, if you occupy your mind with leisure activities, you will be better at making decisions after gaining more insight.
• Downtime is needed to recharge your batteries: During deep work, you use a form of energy called directed attention. It is a limited resource. To recharge, you need to take a break.
• Downtime usually replaces unimportant work: The capacity to burst into deep work on any given day is finite. In most cases, it is only for a couple of hours. Once you have exhausted these hours, any task that comes after is less productive and less critical.
• Downtime is different from shallow work: These activities occupy some part of your attention. They represent leisure, taking a stroll, casually conversing with a friend, playing a game with your kid, or listening to music.

Less mental clutter means more mental resources available for deep thinking. ~ Cal Newport

So, how do you incorporate downtime into a daily routine? Create your own shutdown ritual — close your laptop, write tomorrow's top three priorities on a sticky note, then take a 10-minute walk around the block to clear your head. Don’t check your emails after dinner, stop planning for the next job, and allow no mental replays of conversations. As much as you can, shut down thinking completely.

Cal Newport, for instance, rarely works on weekends and ends his workdays by 5.30 pm. Despite that, he has written many best-selling books, published many scientific papers, and won two competitive grants. So, get into deep work and watch your professional life thrive!

Productivity is not about committing more hours to work but making the most of the hours you do work.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The truth is that deep work is a pain in the neck. It closely connects to our attention and keeps it undivided as long as possible on the same thing.

You must fight distractions continually and shut them down by staying away from social media, limiting your time on the Internet, and planning your workdays and leisure periods in advance.

That all sounds overwhelming, especially for those who grew up in the digital age. Distraction, for us, is synonymous with existence. However, it is a popular misconception, and so is multitasking. This "skill" is very beneficial for a carefully programmed robot but not for a human being. If you are an employer, please stop demanding that your colleagues multitask. As an employee, you should know that you are more productive in less time when you concentrate on one task at a time.

A deep life is not for everyone. For many people, it is too much work, and some are more comfortable with artificial busyness. However, if you want optimal productivity, to deploy your mind to the maximum, and get things done effectively, there’s no better way to pursue deep work and live a deep life.

Try this

The next time you need to get into a deep mode:

• Shut down your social media notifications.
• Make sure to switch to the next task once you have finished the ongoing one. There will be an attention residue — working in a semi-distraction state, which is counterproductive.
• Spend less time on the Internet and reduce time playing games on your telephone or computer.
• Exert maximum productivity from the hour's work, and maximum recovery from your downtime.

When you do this, you’ll find it much easier to switch to deep work whenever necessary.
